publications.title: T-cell receptor specificity maintained by altered thermodynamics.
publications.authors: Madura F, Rizkallah PJ, Miles KM, Holland CJ, Bulek AM, Fuller A, Schauenburg AJ, Miles JJ, Liddy N, Sami M, Li Y, Hossain M, Baker BM, Jakobsen BK, Sewell AK, Cole DK.
publications.executive_summary: This study investigated a melanoma-specific TCR (α24β17) engineered via phage display to achieve 30,000-fold enhanced binding affinity (KD = 0.6 nM) to its cognate MHC-I–peptide complex, compared to the low-affinity wild-type TCR (KD range 100 nM–270 μM). Despite the dramatic increase in overall binding strength mediated primarily through new TCR–MHC contacts, the engineered TCR retained exquisite peptide specificity, remaining sensitive to modifications at every position along the peptide backbone—a hallmark of natural TCR recognition. Thermodynamic analysis revealed that solvation effects play a critical role in maintaining this peptide selectivity, uncoupling the mechanisms of affinity enhancement from those governing sequence discrimination. These findings illuminate how TCRs achieve their characteristic specificity and have implications for rational TCR engineering, particularly for cancer immunotherapy where maintaining peptide discrimination is essential to minimize off-target recognition and autoimmunity risk.

publications.title: Molecular Rules Underpinning Enhanced Affinity Binding of Human T Cell Receptors Engineered for Immunotherapy.
publications.authors: Crean RM, MacLachlan BJ, Madura F, Whalley T, Rizkallah PJ, Holland CJ, McMurran C, Harper S, Godkin A, Sewell AK, Pudney CR, van der Kamp MW, Cole DK.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined engineered T cell receptors (TCRs) designed to bind cancer-associated peptide–MHC-I complexes with dramatically higher affinity than their natural counterparts, comparing crystal structures of enhanced-affinity variants with wild-type precursors and performing molecular dynamics simulations to dissect binding mechanisms. The analysis revealed that million-fold affinity improvements can be achieved without fundamentally altering the native TCR–pHLA binding geometry, instead arising from subtle interface modifications—particularly the introduction of buried hydrophobic residues—whose energetic contributions vary substantially across individual TCRs and often involve additive or allosteric effects beyond the mutated positions. These findings provide molecular principles for rational design of engineered TCRs as immunotherapeutic agents for cancer, addressing a major limitation of natural TCRs, which recognize tumor epitopes with insufficient binding strength to mount effective anti-tumor responses.
